items.correct: A syntactic unit headed by a noun, which may include determiners, adjectives and modifiers — e.g. 'the big red car' is a noun phrase functioning as a single grammatical unit.
items.misconception: A noun phrase is just a noun by itself.
items.random_wrong: A noun phrase is a sentence that contains nouns.
items.why: Noun phrases can be one word ('cats') or many ('the cats that lived next door'). The phrase functions as a unit — subject, object or complement. Students reduce NP to a single noun, missing constituent structure.

items.q: How does Piaget's theory relate language to cognitive development?
items.correct: Language develops as a consequence of cognitive development — children must first develop concepts (object permanence, conservation) before they can express them linguistically. Cognition leads language.
items.misconception: Piaget said language drives cognitive development.
items.random_wrong: Piaget was a behaviourist who emphasised reinforcement.
items.why: Piaget's order is cognition first, then language — opposite of Whorfian linguistic determinism. Vygotsky disagreed, arguing language and thought interact. Students confuse the direction of Piaget's claim.

items.q: What is the difference between a phoneme and a grapheme?
items.correct: A phoneme is the smallest unit of sound in a language; a grapheme is the written letter or letters that represent it.
items.misconception: Phonemes are letters and graphemes are syllables.
items.random_wrong: Phonemes are vowels and graphemes are consonants.
items.why: Phonemes are sounds, graphemes are written symbols. The grapheme 'sh' represents one phoneme; the grapheme 'c' can represent two (/k/ or /s/). Students treat them as the same thing.

items.q: What does a letter to unknown recipient begin with?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Dear Sir/Madam. “Hi” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Topic. “Closing” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many key features (FPA) of transactional writing?
items.why: The real figure is 3 features. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
